title: Quick check: reading an ITDR Incident Report

questions.prompt: What is the main shape difference between EDR Evidence and ITDR Evidence?

choices.label: EDR is event-shaped; ITDR is process-shaped.
choices.rationale: Inverted. The lesson names which surface has which shape.

choices.label: There is no difference; the surfaces share Evidence formats.
choices.rationale: The lesson opens by naming the difference.

choices.label: EDR Evidence is plain English; ITDR Evidence is raw JSON.
choices.rationale: Both surfaces present Evidence in the same Incident Report skeleton.

questions.explanation: ITDR is mostly event-shaped (sign-ins, rule creations, OAuth grants, MFA changes). ITDR Evidence is event-shaped: each event has its own timestamp and source. The story comes from the sequence.

questions.prompt: Which of the three Timeline-reading habits does the lesson name first?

choices.label: Read bottom-to-top to find the most recent activity first.
choices.rationale: The lesson reads chronologically forwards, not backwards.

choices: Read top-to-bottom in order; the events compound.

choices.label: Skip the timestamps and read event types only.
choices.rationale: Timestamps are part of the compromise story. Skipping them loses the speed signal.

choices.label: Read each event in isolation to avoid bias.
choices.rationale: The relationships between events are the point. Isolation hides the chain.

questions.explanation: The order shows how the attacker chained actions. Reading in sequence reveals the causal thread.

questions.prompt: A Timeline has a 40-minute gap between a high-impact attacker action and the next event. What does the lesson say?

choices.label: Gaps mean the attacker has stopped and the compromise is over.
choices.rationale: That is the assumption the lesson warns against.

choices.label: Ignore gaps; only events matter.
choices.rationale: Gaps are signal, especially around high-impact actions.

choices: Gaps do not mean the attacker stopped.

choices.label: Gaps are normal because identity events are bursty by nature.
choices.rationale: Wrong framing for compromise Timelines specifically.

questions.explanation: A clean compromise story rarely has long gaps. The gap is worth flagging. A gap often means the next event fell outside what the SOC ingested. Surface to senior if the picture is unclear.

questions.prompt: Using the Timeline to argue with the Recommendation. What kind of mistake is this?

choices.label: An escalation trigger.
choices.rationale: The action is wrong regardless of escalation status.

choices.label: A legitimate use of the Timeline.
choices.rationale: The Timeline is for context (action and comms), not for litigating the SOC.

choices.label: A documentation issue, not a workflow one.
choices.rationale: This is a workflow mistake.

choices: Same second-guessing failure from the Beginner course.
questions.explanation: The Timeline is what the analyst weighed; the Recommendation is the disposition. The Timeline is context. The Recommendation drives the action. Arguing with the Recommendation using the Timeline is second-guessing the SOC.

questions.prompt: A Timeline shows events across multiple identities in the same tenant. What does the lesson say?

choices.label: Run the playbook on each user in sequence.
choices.rationale: Solo playbook on multiple users lets the attacker continue to move through ones you have not reached.

choices.label: Treat the multi-user pattern as coincidence; each incident is separate.
choices.rationale: The cross-user pattern is the signal.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket to ask which user to handle first.
choices.rationale: The escalation path is the senior, not support.

questions.explanation: Multi-user Timelines push the response above the helpdesk ceiling. Escalate.

questions.prompt: What is the cost of reading ITDR Evidence as a flat list rather than chronologically?

choices.label: Nothing; Evidence is just a list and order does not matter.
choices.rationale: Order is the story on ITDR.

choices: You lose the compromise story and it becomes harder to see which events are related.

choices.label: The Recommendation will not apply unless you read chronologically.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation applies regardless. The cost is in customer-comms quality and playbook mapping.

choices.label: You will miss the customer's organisation name.
choices.rationale: The org name is in the Header, not in the Evidence ordering.

questions.explanation: Chronological reading reveals how the attacker chained actions. Flat reading loses the narrative.

questions.prompt: The customer's manager calls back asking for details to brief the security awareness lead. What is the best response shape?

choices.label: Vague framing: "There was a compromise, we caught it quickly, we have reset everything."
choices.rationale: Missing the specifics the security awareness lead needs.

choices.label: Forward the Incident Report directly to the manager.
choices.rationale: The Incident Report is in security tooling. The manager does not have access and should not be your delivery channel.

choices: Walk through the Timeline with timestamps.

choices.label: Tell the manager the SOC will send a report next week.
choices.rationale: Passes the buck. The verbal walk-through is the right shape now.

questions.explanation: Sign-in, attacker actions, containment start, playbook complete, attacker-active window. The Timeline gives you specific timestamps. The customer hears the speed of the attacker and the speed of the response in the same breath.

questions.prompt: High-severity ITDR on a marketing intern. Timeline: seven events in eleven minutes (sign-in, MFA method, inbox rule, OAuth grant, three emails). Recommendation: full playbook. What is your first move?

choices.label: Investigate the OAuth grant first to see whether the intern signed up for a tool legitimately.
choices.rationale: Second-guessing the SOC. The OAuth grant is in the Timeline as part of the compromise chain.

choices: Execute the Recommendation per the playbook.

choices.label: Wait for the intern to be reachable because she is a junior user.
choices.rationale: Seniority does not gate the response. Even an intern's mailbox phishes everyone she emails.

questions.explanation: Document timestamps. Reach the intern's manager to inform of lockout. Standard ITDR compromise response. The Timeline confirms the pattern. Act per the Recommendation.

questions.prompt: When an ITDR Incident Report lands, what is the entry point for reading it?

choices.label: Evidence first, to build your own theory of the compromise.
choices.rationale: Building your own theory is the second-guessing pattern. Header then Recommendation is the entry.

choices: Header (severity, surface, affected identity), then Recommendation.

choices.label: Timeline first, to understand the sequence before reading the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Timeline is context, not the entry point.

choices.label: Summary first, then Evidence.
choices.rationale: Summary is a fallback when the Recommendation surprises you, not the entry.

questions.explanation: Same reading order as EDR: Header, Recommendation, verify entity, Summary if needed, Evidence and Timeline as context.

questions.prompt: "Skimming the Timeline because the Recommendation tells me what to do anyway." Why is this a mistake?

choices.label: The Recommendation does not apply without reading the Timeline.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation applies regardless. The cost is elsewhere.

choices.label: The Timeline contains information the Recommendation omits.
choices.rationale: Sometimes true, but the primary cost the lesson names is the customer-comms quality.

choices: True for the action, false for the customer comms.

choices.label: Skimming is fine if the playbook is familiar.
choices.rationale: Customer comms always benefit from Timeline specificity, regardless of playbook familiarity.

questions.explanation: The Timeline is what makes the customer call coherent afterwards. The Recommendation drives the action. The Timeline drives the specificity of the post-incident customer conversation.
